elements.sent-2411-001: We study the connection between audio-visual observations and the underlying physics of a mundane yet intriguing everyday activity: pouring liquids.
elements.sent-2411-002: Given only the sound of liquid pouring into a container, our objective is to automatically infer physical properties such as the liquid level, the shape and size of the container, the pouring rate and the time to fill.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we: (i) show in theory that these properties can be determined from the fundamental frequency (pitch); (ii) train a pitch detection model with supervision from simulated data and visual data with a physics-inspired objective; (iii) introduce a new large dataset of real pouring videos for a systematic study; (iv) show that the trained model can indeed infer these physical properties for real data; and finally, (v) we demonstrate strong generalization to various container shapes, other datasets, and in-the-wild YouTube videos.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our work presents a keen understanding of a narrow yet rich problem at the intersection of acoustics, physics, and learning.
elements.sent-2411-005: It opens up applications to enhance multisensory perception in robotic pouring.
elements.sent-2411-006: What can possibly be scientifically interesting about such a mundane chore as pouring a liquid into a glass? We perform this action all the time but barely realise that we effortlessly learn to infer several useful physical properties in the process.
elements.sent-2411-007: For example, evidence in psychoacoustics suggests that humans can accurately infer the level of the liquid, the time to fill~\cite{Cabe2000HumanST , the size of the container~\cite{container_size_doi:10.1177/0956797618813319 , and even the temperature of the liquid~\cite{temperature1_https://doi.org/10.1111/joss.12052,temperature2_Agrawal2020HearingWT,tomscott_youtube , merely from the sound of pouring.}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Such inference (\eg, time to fill) allows us to adaptively control our actions (\eg, stopping pouring to prevent spillage) conforming to the affordance theory by Gibson~\cite{gibson2014ecological .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this work, we study the physical phenomenon involved in liquid pouring and explore how it can be used to train machines to infer useful physical properties from sound alone.
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite its mundaneness, liquid pouring has rich physics underpinning it and has been studied for more than a century~\cite{rayleigh1896theory .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The crux of this exploration is summarized well by Berg and Stork~\cite{berg1982physics : ``As the liquid (\eg water) is filled, a sound consisting of an increasing pitch and some (odd) harmonics superimposed with whooshing, gurgling is observed''.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This pitch and the corresponding harmonics are a function of the physical properties.
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, the shape of the pitch depends on the container shape~\cite{radial1-French1983InVV, webster2010use, radial2-PhysRev.13.171 , the range of the pitch depends on the container dimensions~\cite{Cabe2000HumanST , and the rate change of pitch depends on the pouring flow rate\cite{Cabe2000HumanST .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Thus, automatically inferring useful physical properties from the sound of pouring necessitates two stages: (i) detecting the pitch from the raw audio signal, and (ii) recovering these physical properties from the pitch.
elements.sent-2411-015: There are several challenges in training machines to do these purely from sound.
elements.sent-2411-016: First, such a task requires a fine-grained, time-sensitive understanding of audio while contemporary models focus more on coarse recognition tasks like sound classification~\cite{kay2017kinetics,vggsound,audioset .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Second, the underlying physics of such a niche activity as pouring is not fully developed for a general container and liquid setup, unlike, say, Newtonian mechanics studied analogously in \cite{phys101 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Third, there is a lack of clean, controlled, and large datasets which are necessary to study physical property estimation by learning.
elements.sent-2411-019: Fourth, supervision either in the form of pitch annotation or the actual physical properties is difficult to obtain and to use directly in training.
elements.sent-2411-020: To enable a systematic study, we collect a clean and large dataset of 805 videos of pouring across 50 diverse containers.
elements.sent-2411-021: For training, we select a subset of containers shaped like cylinders such that we can approximate the underlying physics with that of a cylinder~\cite{Cabe2000HumanST .}
elements.sent-2411-022: We design an audio network for pitch detection based on wav2vec2~\cite{baevski2020wav2vec pre-trained on speech data that has characteristic pitch dynamics~\cite{morise2017harvest .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: As pitch annotations are hard and ambiguous to obtain at scale, we use supervision from simulated data and visual data.
elements.sent-2411-024: We pre-train the network on simulated sounds of liquid pouring.
elements.sent-2411-025: On real data, we fine-tune the network by visual co-supervision with a physics-inspired objective.
elements.sent-2411-026: We demonstrate that the co-supervised audio model is able to predict pitch, and hence estimate physical properties, with a performance far exceeding that of multiple previous methods.
elements.sent-2411-027: Why is this important, though? First, as far as we know, this is the first work to demonstrate human-like capabilities (or better) in predicting physical properties from sound alone -- in fact, we achieve an accuracy of $\pm$\SI{0.60 {\centi\meter in predicting the air column height for cylinders.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Second, although the model is trained on cylinders, we show that the pitch estimation (and the model in general) is applicable beyond cylinders -- for example, it can be used to predict the shape of containers with convincing accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-029: Third, the model generalizes well to videos from other datasets and to in-the-wild YouTube videos.
elements.sent-2411-031: We show in theory that the physical properties of the container-liquid system can be recovered from the fundamental frequency (pitch) of the sound of pouring.
elements.sent-2411-032: We train a pitch detection model using supervision from simulated data and visual data with a physics-inspired objective.
elements.sent-2411-033: We introduce a new clean and large dataset of videos of liquid pouring that can be used to study the estimation of physical properties.

elements.sent-2411-132: We want to train the audio network described in \cref{subsec:audio-network-arch to detect pitch.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Since it is difficult to obtain pitch annotations on real samples, we first pre-train the network on synthetic samples with perfect ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-134: Here, we describe synthetic data generation and the details of pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-135: We train a generative model based on Differentiable Digital Signal Processing (DDSP)~\cite{engel2020ddsp to simulate the sounds of liquid pouring.}
elements.sent-2411-136: The architecture is a supervised autoencoder model with the latent space decomposed into pitch, loudness, and a residual vector.
elements.sent-2411-137: The encoder represents the raw audio waveform in three parts: (i) pitch over time, (ii) loudness over time, and (iii) residual vector.
elements.sent-2411-138: The pitch is extracted using CREPE~\cite{kim2018crepe , a standard pitch detector popularly used in music.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Loudness is extracted as standard RMS energy.
elements.sent-2411-140: The residual is learned with a GRU network operating on Mel Frequency Cepstrum Coefficients (MFCC) features.
elements.sent-2411-141: Intuitively, the residual captures background noise and room reverberation characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-142: The decoder is composed of synthesizers based on classical signal processing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-143: It takes in pitch, loudness, and the residual and generates a realistic waveform.
elements.sent-2411-144: The network is trained using a multi-scale spectrogram reconstruction loss.
elements.sent-2411-145: To generate a sample, we first randomly pick a conditioning sample from the train set and extract loudness and residual vector from it.
elements.sent-2411-146: We can pass an arbitrary pitch profile with the chosen loudness and residual.
elements.sent-2411-147: To simute a pitch profile, we sample dimensions of an arbitrary cylindrical container.
elements.sent-2411-148: Formally, we sample radius $R$, height $H$, and then compute length of air column as a linear curve: \begin{equation}l(t) = \left(-\frac{H}{T}\right)t + H,\end{equation} where $T$ is the duration of the conditioning sample.
elements.sent-2411-149: Then, we plug this in \cref{eq:wavelength-length to compute the wavelength $\lambda(t)$ which is inverted to obtain the pitch.}
elements.sent-2411-150: These three (pitch, loudness, residual) are then fed to the trained decoder which generates a realistic waveform with the desired pitch profile.
elements.sent-2411-151: For a single conditioning sample, the loudness and residual are fixed while we can vary $(R, H)$ to vary the pitch and cover a vast diversity of pitch profiles.
elements.sent-2411-152: Some examples of generated samples are shown in \cref{fig:synthetic-samples-main .}
elements.sent-2411-153: We sample $H \sim U[5, 25]$ cm, $R \sim U[1, 5]$ cm and generate $10{, 000$ samples.}

elements.sent-2411-162: The pre-trained audio network needs to overcome the sim2real gap to detect pitch accurately on real samples.
elements.sent-2411-163: Since it is difficult to obtain ground-truth pitch annotations on real samples, we make use of the video stream as a source of weak supervision for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-164: From the RHS in \cref{eq:wavelength-length , the video can supply measurements for the length of the air column and the container radius (up to a scale), which we use to supervise the audio network.}
elements.sent-2411-165: To use video as a teacher for co-supervision, we pre-train a video network to detect the length of the air column (or equivalently, the level of liquid) and the average radius of the container.
elements.sent-2411-167: We use a frozen DINO~\cite{caron2021emerging encoder per frame and attach a Transformer to model the temporal dependencies.}
elements.sent-2411-168: This is followed by a prediction head for the length of the air column $l(t)$ (relative to the image size).

elements.sent-2411-174: To enable video as a weak supervisor, we need to account for a scale factor, $\alpha$. \begin{equation}\alpha. \left(\frac{\lambda(t)}{4}\right) = l_{px}(t) + \beta R_{px} \label{eq:cosupervision}\end{equation} This factor encapsulates the depth and camera intrinsics and is unique per video.
elements.sent-2411-175: Assuming a usual perspective camera, we can obtain a precise definition of the scale factor: \begin{equation}\alpha := \frac{1}{Z}\frac{f}{s}, \label{eq:alpha}\end{equation} where $Z$ is the depth, $f$ is the focal length and $s$ is length per pixel.
elements.sent-2411-176: Note that $\alpha$ is inversely proportional to depth.
elements.sent-2411-177: We can pre-compute $\alpha$ for each video by simply computing the ratio of wavelength from audio to the pixel lengths from video.
elements.sent-2411-178: However, since the audio estimates are generally off where the signal is low (e.g., towards the end of the pouring sequence), this leads to very poor estimates of $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-179: To account for this, we weigh the ratios over time with the RMS energy in the audio over time.
elements.sent-2411-180: This leads to robust scale estimates.

elements.sent-2411-208: In this section, we present various experiments to demonstrate physical understanding from sounds of liquid pouring.
elements.sent-2411-209: We start with a brief description of our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-210: Then, we describe the results on estimating pitch (\cref{tab:pitch-detection-results ) and physical properties from pitch (\cref{tab:physical-properties ).}}
elements.sent-2411-211: Then, we present results on classifying container shapes and estimating liquid weight directly from the learned representations.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Estimating basic physical properties from pouring sound
elements.sent-2411-212: As theoretically shown in \cref{subsec:theory , we now experimentally show estimation of physical properties from pouring sound.}
elements.sent-2411-213: We evaluate our pitch detection network to estimate the air column length.
elements.sent-2411-214: We compare against simpler baselines for pitch detection.
elements.sent-2411-215: We try classical pitch detectors like Yin~\cite{de2002yin , CNN-based detectors like CREPE~\cite{kim2018crepe and recent self-supervised detectors like PESTO~\cite{riou2023pesto .}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: We also try a baseline with per-frame argmax on the spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-217: Note that other baselines estimate pitch directly from raw audio samples while the argmax method operates on a spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-218: Given pitch (fundamental wavelengths) at certain time points, we fit a line to estimate $\lambda(t)$ on the collection of obtained points using RANSAC to avoid outliers.
elements.sent-2411-219: To compute length of air column from wavelengths, we rely on \cref{eq:length .}
elements.sent-2411-220: We compare our models with the baselines in estimating $l(t)$ and report the mean absolute error averaged over all time points.
elements.sent-2411-222: Our models outperform all the baselines.
elements.sent-2411-223: Moreover, the co-supervised model achieves an error of 0.60 cm as compared to the audio-only variant's 0.78 cm on Test set I.
elements.sent-2411-224: The results are similar on the more challenging Test set II.
elements.sent-2411-225: Having shown the superiority of our pitch detection model, we now check how much visual co-supervision helps in estimating other physical properties in \cref{tab:physical-properties .}
elements.sent-2411-226: We obtain the container radius $R$ and height $H$ using \cref{eq:container-dimensions .}
elements.sent-2411-227: The ground truths are obtained by manually measuring the said containers.
elements.sent-2411-228: Our best model achieves an MAE of 2.27 cm in height and 1.39 cm in radius on Test set I and 2.77 cm / 1.88 cm on Test set II.
elements.sent-2411-229: Note that radius measurement depends on reliable wavelength estimates towards the end of the audio which is significantly better in case of a co-supervised model than the audio-only variant.
elements.sent-2411-230: This is further shown qualitatively and quantitatively in \cref{fig:cosupervised-vs-audioonly .}
elements.sent-2411-232: Following \cref{eq:flowrate , we evaluate volume flow rate prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-233: This is dependent on $R$ and derivative of $\lambda(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-234: The ground truth is obtained using the ratio of the volume of container and the time it took to fill it completely.
elements.sent-2411-235: The co-supervised model achieves 22.5 ml/s compared to 25.2 ml/s of the audio-only model on Test set I.
elements.sent-2411-236: Similar improvement is observed on Test set II.
elements.sent-2411-238: Following \cref{eq:ttf , we evaluate on time to fill prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-239: We are given a partial audio, i.e. cut at 25\% or 50\% or 75\% of its original duration and the task is to predict time (in seconds) that it will require to fill the container.
elements.sent-2411-240: Since we vary the flow rates randomly, it prevents the obvious shortcut of simply extrapolating based on the given time.
elements.sent-2411-241: As shown in \cref{tab:physical-properties , the co-supervised model is comparable to the audio-only when provided with only 25\% of the input but outperforms the latter given 50\% or 75\% of the input on Test set I.}
elements.sent-2411-242: On Test set II, the co-supervised model outperforms at all levels of the input.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Recognizing liquid container shape from its pouring sound
elements.sent-2411-243: Differently shaped containers exhibit different structures of the resonance curves.
elements.sent-2411-244: Sound of pouring in cylindrical containers show a pitch that varies as $1/l(t)$ where $l(t)$ is the length of air column~\cite{Cabe2000HumanST .}
elements.sent-2411-245: In contrast, in bottles, pitch varies as $1/\sqrt{l(t)}$~\cite{webster2010use .}
elements.sent-2411-246: Though we train for pitch detection only on cylinder-like containers, here we evaluate whether the learned representations encode container shape.
elements.sent-2411-247: Formally, given the sequence of features of the sound of pouring, the task is to classify whether the shape of the container is cylindrical, semiconical, or bottleneck.
elements.sent-2411-248: Given the sound of pouring, we first compute features from the co-supervised audio encoder.
elements.sent-2411-249: These are outputs, say $\{\mathbf{z _{i}\}_{i=1 ^{N}$, from the last Transformer block of wav2vec2 before going into the prediction head in \cref{fig:stage1-arch .}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: Then, we concatenate the following vectors to create a single summary vector per sample: the mean of the sequence and each of the vectors at 25\%, 50\% and 75\% of the sequence length. \begin{equation}\mathbf{z}_{\text{summary}} = concat\left( \mathbb{E}_{t}[\mathbf{z}_{t}], \mathbf{z}_{\frac{N}{4}}, \mathbf{z}_{\frac{N}{2}}, \mathbf{z}_{\frac{3N}{4}} \right),\end{equation} where $N$ is the sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-251: We attach a 3-way linear shape classifier head to this vector.
elements.sent-2411-252: To report performance, we consider the Test Set III set of our dataset consisting of 434 videos (227 semiconical, 107 bottleneck and 100 cylindrical).
elements.sent-2411-253: These are unseen samples not part of the training and evaluation set used previously to estimate physical properties.
elements.sent-2411-254: We split these randomly in an 80-20 split.
elements.sent-2411-255: On this split, we achieve a per sample accuracy of 90.91\% and a mean class accuracy of 92.47\%.
elements.sent-2411-256: In comparison, features without co-supervision achieve 88.63\% and 89.44\%.
elements.sent-2411-257: This shows: (i) a model trained to detect pitch implicitly encodes container shape, and (ii) co-supervision further improves shape recognition.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{media/shape-prediction-results-v3.pdf} \captionsetup{font=normal,skip=1pt} \caption{Results on shape recognition from audio embeddings. (a) Latent embeddings learned by the co-supervised audio model encode container shape. (b) On an unseen test set, it is able to recognize the container shapes with a sample accuracy of 90.91\% and mean class accuracy of 92.47\%. Some semiconical containers with small difference between the top and base radius are essentially cylindrical and that shows in the t-SNE embeddings. } \label{fig:shape-prediction}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Estimating liquid weight from its pouring sound
elements.sent-2411-258: The weight of the liquid being poured is a function of its density (constant) and the container volume it occupies.
elements.sent-2411-259: Here, we evaluate whether the learned audio representations can be regressed to predict the weight of the liquid.
elements.sent-2411-260: Since our dataset does not have weight annotations, we evaluate on the dataset proposed by \citet{Wilson2019AnalyzingLP .}
elements.sent-2411-261: While \cite{Wilson2019AnalyzingLP directly fine-tune on their dataset, we only linearly probe representations from our co-supervised network.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Given input sound, we cut 0.4s snippets and compute a sequence of features $\{\mathbf{z _{i}\}_{i=1 ^{N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-263: We attach a linear regressor head which outputs a scalar weight for each feature vector $\mathbf{z _{i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-264: For transparent comparison, we report comparison with the same baselines, on the same data and splits as in \cite{Wilson2019AnalyzingLP .}
elements.sent-2411-265: Specifically, it consists of 136 video sequences with weight annotations across six different containers and two liquids.
elements.sent-2411-266: Following \cite{Wilson2019AnalyzingLP , for each container, we train a separate regressor on its own split and report performance in \cref{tab:mass-est .}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Our model outperforms all the baselines as well as the strong supervised baseline in \cite{Wilson2019AnalyzingLP .}
elements.sent-2411-268: On average, our model achieves an MAE of 1.20 oz which is the best amongst all baselines.
elements.sent-2411-269: Not surprisingly, our model performs the best with a cylindrical container (Column 4) but also is impressive on a bottleneck container (Columns 5 and 6).
elements.sent-2411-270: This shows that a network trained for pitch detection learns features capable of estimating liquid mass which is a function of the container volume and liquid density.
elements.sent-2411-271: In \cref{appendix-subsec:other-results , we also evaluate generalization across containers where we train a mass regressor on sounds of one container and evaluate it on those of another container.}

elements.sent-2411-284: In this work, we considered the case of pouring water in a container and analysed the connection between the underlying physics and audio-visual observations.
elements.sent-2411-285: While humans can reason well about the physical properties (e.g., absolute container size) merely from the sound of pouring, we have shown early evidence of training machines to achieve similar capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-286: We developed synthetic data to pre-train an audio network for pitch detection.
elements.sent-2411-287: We fine-tuned it on real data with no external supervision thanks to the co-supervision from the video stream.

elements.sent-2411-322: The audio network architecture is based on wav2vec2~\cite{baevski2020wav2vec .}
elements.sent-2411-323: It takes in raw audio samples $\mathbf{x \in \mathbb{R ^{N}$ and outputs a distribution over wavelengths $\mathbf{y \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times K}$ where $T$ is the number of time frames and $K$ is the number of wavelength bins.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-324: The network consists of (i) a feature encoder that converts raw samples to feature vectors and (ii) a transformer encoder to capture information from the entire sequence.
elements.sent-2411-325: The feature encoder is a 1D CNN: $\mathbb{R ^{N \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{T \times d}$ where $T$ is the number of frames.}}}
elements.sent-2411-326: The CNN encoder processes each frame of $400$ samples with a hop length of $320$.

elements.sent-2411-328: It consists of seven blocks and the temporal convolutions in each block have 512 channels with strides (5,2,2,2,2,2,2) and kernel widths (10,3,3,3,3,2,2).
elements.sent-2411-329: Each block has a layer normalization and a GELU activation function~\cite{hendrycks2016gaussian .}

elements.sent-2411-334: To co-supervise the audio network, we first train a visual network to detect the length of the air column $l(t)$ and the radius of the container $R$.
elements.sent-2411-335: Consider a video with $F$ frames of liquid pouring.
elements.sent-2411-336: Since the camera and the container are static, the temporal difference between consecutive frames serves as a strong signal for the liquid height at a given time.
elements.sent-2411-337: First, for each frame, we black out pixels outside the container using the segmentation map.
elements.sent-2411-338: Then, we compute temporal difference maps simply by computing the normalized pixel-wise difference between consecutive frames.
elements.sent-2411-339: We take the mean over image width which gives as a single map of size $F \times H$ where $H$ is the image height.
elements.sent-2411-340: These are Gaussian smoothened over $F$ and $H$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-342: Then, for each frame, we pick the highest intensity ordinate shown as green scatter points in \cref{fig:tdm (d).}
elements.sent-2411-343: To avoid noise, we fit a second-order polynomial with RANSAC to these points as shown in \cref{fig:tdm (e).}
elements.sent-2411-344: The container height minus this fit curve gives us a ground truth estimate of $l(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-345: These are then used to train a DINO based video network.
elements.sent-2411-346: We train a deep network with these ground truths because it can generalize better to unseen containers and other variations over the handcrafted method.
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{media/tdm-explainer-v1.pdf} \captionsetup{skip=1mm} \caption{Obtaining pseudo labels for visual pre-training. For a given video (sample frame at time $t$ in (a)), we mask out the container (b) and then compute pixel wise differences which leads to a tensor of size $F \times H \times W$. We average across width and get a temporal difference map (c). We fit a polynomial with RANSAC (e) to the argmax points (d) which gives us an estimate for $l(t), \forall t$.} \label{fig:tdm}
elements.sent-2411-347: Given the strong dense visual understanding of DINO~\cite{caron2021emerging , we use it as an image backbone and adapt it to detect length of air column in videos.}
elements.sent-2411-348: For each frame, we extract the CLS token features out of DINO.
elements.sent-2411-349: The sequence of CLS tokens is projected to a lower dimension and passed through to a light Transformer to contextualize features.
elements.sent-2411-351: The Transformer has a single layer with 4 heads and 128 dimensions.
